sources.name: Wikipedia — Demon's Crest
sources.notes: 6 crest system, Metroidvania structure, Firebrand mechanics.

notes: Demon's Crest is the third and final game in the Gargoyle's Quest series. Firebrand's ultimate game.
DEFINING MECHANIC: The Crest system — 6 crests that transform Firebrand's abilities. A Metroidvania built around form upgrades.
Retains wall cling and hover from Gargoyle's Quest II but dramatically expands with new forms.
The crests don't just give new attacks — they change Firebrand's physics and traversal capabilities.
True ending only accessible by collecting all 6 crests before facing the final boss.
Features a shop (Dr. Phibes) and multiple endings based on crest collection.
Considered an underrated SNES gem — commercially unsuccessful but critically acclaimed decades later.

base_physics._note: Firebrand's default (Fire Crest) physics. Foundation for all other crest variations.

walk_speed.notes: Deliberate pace — Firebrand is powerful, not fast. VERIFY.

wall_cling.description: Grab vertical surfaces. Climb up, descend slowly, launch off.
wall_cling.notes: Retained from Gargoyle's Quest II — same mechanic.

hover.description: Press jump again in air — flap wings for limited hover.
hover.stamina: Wing stamina bar depletes during hover. Refills on ground or wall contact.
hover.notes: Retained and improved from Gargoyle's Quest II.

crest_system._note: THE DEFINING MECHANIC. 6 crests = 6 different Firebrand transformations. Metroidvania structure.

fire_crest.description: Default form. Standard fireball attack, hover, wall cling.
fire_crest.notes: Starting form. All other crests build on this.

new_capabilities: Walk through spike traps without damage
Destroy cracked rock walls by charging into them
earth_crest.notes: Opens previously inaccessible areas with spike floors and rock walls.

new_capabilities: Enhanced hover — longer duration and more altitude control
Some sources describe this as enabling true flight rather than hover
heaven_crest.notes: Dramatically expands vertical traversal. Some ceiling-areas only accessible in Heaven form.

new_capabilities: Slows time in the environment — Firebrand moves at normal speed, enemies move slower
time_crest.notes: The time slowdown is a situational tool — activatable, not constant.

water_crest.notes: Opens underwater areas. VERIFY specific capabilities.

infinity_crest.description: Found in a specific hidden location. Not required for standard ending.
infinity_crest.notes: Secret form for players who explore thoroughly.

all_crests.description: Collecting all 6 crests unlocks the true final form.
new_capabilities: Access to true final boss and true ending
all_crests.notes: Required for the true ending. Multiple playthroughs may be needed to find all crests.

fireball.description: Firebrand's primary projectile. Crest determines element and behavior.
fireball.notes: Each crest changes the fireball's appearance, element, and potentially trajectory.

shop.notes: The shop enables persistent progression between stages — currency saved across play sessions.

endings.standard_ending: Defeat final boss without all crests — reveals incomplete story.
endings.true_ending: Collect all 6 crests including Infinity Crest, then defeat true final boss — full story.
endings.notes: Multiple ending system incentivizes thorough exploration.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Demon's Crest is the pinnacle of the Gargoyle's Quest design language — the same wall-cling/hover foundation from GQ2 becomes a launchpad for six distinct physical identities. The 'which crest is best for this area?' question is the game's central puzzle, applied not to a puzzle switch but to Firebrand's own body.

The Earth Crest's ability to walk through spikes is a profound shift in threat perception: spike floors that previously defined an impassable boundary become walkable terrain. The player's mental map of 'danger zones' must be redrawn with each new crest. Environment meaning is crest-dependent.

Demon's Crest's commercial failure (despite critical appreciation) is a case study in timing and marketing: it released against Donkey Kong Country in holiday 1994 — one of the most technically impressive games of the era. The comparison was impossible to survive commercially.

The true ending requirement (collect all 6 crests first) respects the player who explores thoroughly. Casual players get a complete but lesser experience. Thorough players get the true story. This is depth rewarding depth — the game recognizes different engagement levels and gives proportional payoff.

Firebrand as protagonist across three games (GQ, GQ2, DC) represents Capcom's willingness to invest in a 'villain protagonist' franchise in the late 16-bit era. By Demon's Crest, Firebrand had more mechanical depth than most hero characters of his era.
